metadata.author: Juan Antonio Breña Moral
metadata.title: Micronaut Security Guidelines
metadata.description: Use when you need to design, review, or improve security in Micronaut applications — including micronaut-security authentication, @Secured and intercept-url-map rules, JWT/session strategies, SecurityService checks, CORS, CSRF awareness for browser apps, rejection handlers, and sensitive-data-safe logging.

prompt.role: You are a Senior software engineer with extensive experience in Micronaut Security and secure API design
prompt.goal: Apply secure-by-default Micronaut security practices: authenticate requests consistently, authorize with explicit role or expression rules, avoid custom header secrets, configure CORS narrowly, and prevent credential and token leakage in logs and responses. Align with `@502-frameworks-micronaut-rest` for HTTP error bodies.

constraints.constraints-description: Before applying recommendations, ensure the project compiles. Compilation failure is a blocking condition.

constraint-list.constraint: **MANDATORY**: Run `./mvnw compile` or `mvn compile` before applying any change
**VERIFY**: Run `./mvnw clean verify` or `mvn clean verify` after applying improvements

example.example-description: Use `SecurityRule.IS_ANONYMOUS`, `IS_AUTHENTICATED`, and role strings (e.g. `ROLE_ADMIN`) on controller classes or methods. Prefer declarative security over manual header checks.
Define `micronaut.security.intercept-url-map` entries for static path policies (health, swagger UI, admin prefixes). Order patterns from specific to general where the implementation requires it.

good-example.code-block: micronaut: security: intercept-url-map: - pattern: /health httpMethod: GET access: - isAnonymous() - pattern: /api/admin/** access: - ROLE_ADMIN - pattern: /api/** access: - isAuthenticated()

bad-example.code-block: pattern: /** access: - isAnonymous() # Entire application including mutating APIs is public

example-header.example-subtitle: Signatures, issuers, and secrets from environment

example.example-description: Configure `micronaut.security.token.jwt` with signatures or JWKS. Load secrets via environment variables or Micronaut Kubernetes config — never commit private keys or symmetric secrets.

good-example.code-block: micronaut: security: token: jwt: signatures: secret: generator: secret: "${JWT_GENERATOR_SIGNATURE_SECRET}" claims-validators: issuer: https://idp.example.com/

example-header.example-subtitle: HttpRequestAuthenticationProvider with roles; never log credentials

example.example-description: Implement `HttpRequestAuthenticationProvider<B>` (Micronaut 4+) to validate credentials against a secure store and return an `AuthenticationResponse` with principal and roles. Never log passwords or API keys.

bad-example.code-block: LOG.info("login attempt password={}", password); // Credential leakage via logs

example-header.example-subtitle: Restrict allowed origins for browser clients

example.example-description: Enable CORS in `micronaut.server.cors` with explicit origins. Avoid `*` when cookies or credentials are sent.

good-example.code-block: micronaut: server: cors: enabled: true configurations: web: allowedOrigins: - https://app.example.com allowedMethods: - GET - POST - PUT - DELETE allowedHeaders: - Authorization - Content-Type

bad-example.code-block: allowedOrigins: - "*" allowedCredentials: true # Invalid or unsafe combination in browsers

example.example-description: Register an `ExceptionHandler` for `io.micronaut.security.authentication.AuthorizationException` and inspect `isForbidden()` to return the correct status: 401 for unauthenticated callers and 403 for authenticated callers that lack the required role.

bad-example.code-block: return HttpResponse.status(403).body(e.toString()); // Always 403 regardless of auth state; leaks exception detail

example-header.example-subtitle: Browser-facing apps need explicit CSRF strategy

example.example-description: Pure bearer-token APIs are often immune to classic CSRF. If Micronaut serves cookie-based sessions to browsers, enable CSRF protection or use same-site cookies and modern SPA patterns; do not assume `@Secured` alone prevents CSRF.

good-example.code-block: Document: cookie session login uses CSRF tokens or SameSite=strict cookies; APIs use Authorization header only.

bad-example.code-block: Cookie session for SPA + state-changing POST without CSRF defense — vulnerable to cross-site form posts

output-format-list.output-format-item: **ANALYZE** `@Secured` coverage, intercept URL maps, JWT config, CORS, and error responses for authz failures
**CATEGORIZE** issues: anonymous-by-default, weak JWT secrets, overbroad CORS, missing role checks, information disclosure
**APPLY** least-privilege rules, secure token configuration, and JSON/Problem responses for 401/403
**VALIDATE** with `./mvnw compile` before and `./mvnw clean verify` after changes

safeguards-list.safeguards-item: Do not commit signing secrets or API keys to the repository
Do not log Authorization headers or JWTs
Re-verify security rules after dependency upgrades to micronaut-security
